did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning and machine learning pipeline for early skin cancer detection. Specifically, it employs a pretrained Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for deep feature extraction, Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering for lesion segmentation, and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) for classifying Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, and Keratosis using fused handcrafted and deep features on the ISIC-2020 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a novel hybrid classification pipeline (CLAHE-enhanced FCM-CNN-SVM) for early skin cancer detection using the ISIC-2020 dataset. It details original methodology including preprocessing, segmentation, feature extraction, and classification steps, and reports original experimental results with specific performance metrics (e.g., 91.89% accuracy) compared against existing models. Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE SCEECS), it contains original experimental validation and new results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Selective Clustering Based Deep Learning System for Early Skin Cancer Detection Using ISIC-2020
journal: 2026 IEEE International Students' Conference on Electrical, Electronics, and Computer Science
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep learning pipeline for early skin cancer detection using the ISIC-2020 dataset, focusing on classifying Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, and Keratosis. The methodology integrates Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for preprocessing, Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering for lesion segmentation, and a pretrained Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for deep feature extraction. These deep features are fused with handcrafted features, including GLCM, color, and shape descriptors, and classified using a Support Vector Machine (SVM). The model was evaluated against baseline U-Net and traditional CNN architectures using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed method achieved a classification accuracy of 91.89%, outperforming U-Net (87.6%) and traditional CNNs (86.2%). The findings confirm the effectiveness of combining enhanced preprocessing, selective clustering, and hybrid feature fusion for robust dermatological diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three clinically significant classes selected from the ISIC-2020 dataset for training and evaluation. Used in classification and segmentation tasks.
One of the three clinically significant classes selected from the ISIC-2020 dataset for training and evaluation. Used in classification and segmentation tasks.

One of the three clinically significant classes selected from the ISIC-2020 dataset for training and evaluation. Referred to as 'Keratosis' in methodology and 'Seborrheic Keratosis' in Figure 4 description.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly describes a 'hybrid classification pipeline' and 'classification accuracy' of 91.89%. The model predicts discrete class labels for three specific skin lesion types using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier on fused features. While segmentation is performed as a preprocessing step using FCM, the primary predictive task is multi-class classification.

Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering is used for 'precise lesion segmentation' to extract the Region of Interest (ROI) before feature extraction. This is a preprocessing step for the classification task, not the primary prediction task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2020
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset containing various types of skin lesions, used as the primary source for training and evaluating the proposed hybrid classification pipeline.

use.training: Used to train the SVM classifier with fused handcrafted and deep features.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the model's performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and compare against U-Net and traditional CNN baselines.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) without statistical significance analysis.

ISIC-2020.notes: The proposed model outperformed U-Net and traditional CNN baselines. Table 1 provides specific GLCM texture features (Contrast, Energy, Homogeneity, Entropy, Correlation) for six representative images (Image1-Image6) across Melanoma, Nevus, and Keratosis classes. Figure 6 shows ROC curves with AUC scores for the three classes. Figure 5 presents the confusion matrix.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) used for feature extraction; specific backbone architecture (e.g., ResNet, VGG) not specified in the provided text.

architecture_details.segmentation: Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering to extract Region of Interest (ROI)

handcrafted: GLCM features (Contrast, Energy, Homogeneity, Entropy, Correlation)
Color features (Mean and standard deviation of RGB and HSV channels)
feature_extraction.deep: High-dimensional abstract features extracted via pretrained CNN

architecture_details.fusion: Concatenation of handcrafted features and deep CNN features

preprocessing_techniques: Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for enhancing local contrast and highlighting skin lesion regions
Median filter for noise reduction (removing salt-and-pepper noise) while preserving edges
Min-max normalization to scale pixel intensity values to the [0, 1] range
Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering for precise lesion segmentation to extract the Region of Interest (ROI)
conclusions: The proposed hybrid CLAHE-enhanced FCM-CNN-SVM pipeline achieves a classification accuracy of 91.89% on the ISIC-2020 dataset, outperforming U-Net (87.6%) and traditional CNN models (86.2%).

The integration of Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for preprocessing, Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering for segmentation, and the fusion of handcrafted (GLCM, color, shape) and deep features extracted via a pretrained CNN significantly improves diagnostic accuracy.
The method demonstrates robust segmentation quality and enhanced lesion visibility, effectively handling noisy or low-contrast dermoscopic images.
The approach enables robust and interpretable classification across three significant skin lesion types: Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, and Keratosis.
The study confirms the effectiveness and generalizability of the proposed approach for automated dermatological diagnosis compared to existing models.
important_remarks: The study is limited to three specific lesion classes (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Keratosis) from the ISIC-2020 dataset, lacking evaluation on other skin conditions or broader dermatological diversity.

No cross-dataset validation was performed; the model was trained and tested solely on the ISIC-2020 dataset, raising concerns about generalizability to external populations or different imaging devices.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) for the performance differences between the proposed model and baselines (U-Net, traditional CNN).
Specific details regarding the pretrained CNN backbone architecture, input image dimensions, and hyperparameter settings are not reported, limiting reproducibility.
The dataset split ratios (training/validation/testing) and class distribution counts are not provided, making it difficult to assess potential class imbalance or data leakage risks.
The work is published in a student conference proceedings (IEEE SCEECS), which may indicate a lower level of peer review rigor compared to top-tier journals or major conferences.
The authors acknowledge that the current model is not optimized for real-time deployment or edge devices, noting computational efficiency as a future research direction.
The system relies exclusively on dermoscopic images, ignoring potential multimodal inputs (e.g., clinical metadata, ultrasound) that could enhance diagnostic accuracy.
No evaluation against adversarial examples or spoofing attacks was conducted, leaving security risks in practical deployment scenarios unaddressed.
